text: The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk suture to create a purse-string suture on the dilated proximal bowel, preparing to decompress it. The colotomy is tied off, and the uterus is suspended in preparation for the bowel resection.

The surgeon identifies the adherence of the colon to the left adnexa and begins dissection from medial to lateral, starting underneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery over the sacral promontory. The left colic artery is visible, and the surgeon plans to preserve it while dissecting around the superior hemorrhoidal artery before proceeding with lateral mobilization.

The surgeon performs a dissection to take down the adherence of the colon to the left adnexa, noting the large and redundant colon. The dissection is completed without the need to mobilize the flexure, as the diverticular strictures are scarred against the left sidewall.

The surgeon performs a standard sigmoid resection, examining the planned distal margin and bowel caliber. A rectal dissection is conducted to facilitate the anastomosis and transect the rectal mesentery. The proximal mesentery is then addressed, with an emphasis on preserving the left colic and sigmoidal branches. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess vascular supply, and two 60mm staplers are employed to transect the chronically dilated bowel.

The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis on the rectal side, using a two-layer technique. The outer row is sewn with two-O PDS sutures, starting at the confluence of the staple lines on the proximal colon side to ensure proper alignment. The proximal colon is offset to the patient's left, and the two-layer end-to-end anastomosis is completed. The surgeon checks ICG for perfusion, particularly on the inner lip, and uses a three-O V-lock suture for the inner row.

We come across, I put a little specimen down in there, now we come across and get our next suture to start and posterior midline. Overall, I've been very happy with how this case has gone. It was very helpful that this was a thin female patient with a nice wide pelvis. The complexity of the case really wasn't that challenging. It was more just decision making. And again, I did elect to make an underverted anastomosis. I did not leave a drain. And the patient did very well. Of course, if the outcome was different. I would blame my decision for having even made it in anastomosis. I think that for all of us at a certain point in our career, we just start to get comfortable. But I also think that from my own personal experience, seeing videos of similar cases done by other surgeons where they did something that I personally wouldn't have done at that point in my career, did give me the confidence to go ahead and try it myself as well as the discussions on this group and others where people have watched my videos and said nice case no need for an ostomy go ahead and make an anastomosis so um Although this might not have been a case in the past where I would have done what you're seeing me do here by making an anastomosis in this heavy smoker with a chronically obstructed proximal bowel and the size discrepancy, it is something that I'm very comfortable with now. That is not to say that this patient isn't a higher risk for a leak, and this was not one of my patients selected for same day or early discharge. I kept them a full three days.

The surgeon uses a 2-O PDS suture to complete the outer anterior row of the anastomosis, tying it to the posterior PDS to ensure a complete two-layer anastomosis. The surgical field is cleaned, and a leak test is performed with a scope, confirming the anastomosis is widely patent with no bleeding or bubbles. Sutures are tidied, and the specimen is prepared for extraction.
